text: Fluency
Confident
Effortless
To stumble
To elaborate
Pace
To express
To keep up
Tongue-tied
To get across
Breakthrough
To sustain
I forgot the word -- it is the thing you use to cook.
That is a good question -- give me a second.
It was great. What about you?
I love it, for example, when it saves me time.
Hi Greg. You will not believe it -- I spoke English on the phone for ten minutes today and I did not translate once.
That is fantastic, Ana Paula! So, how did it feel to just speak freely?
Honestly, almost effortless. I forgot one word, but I described it instead of stopping.
Perfect. That is exactly the trick. What was the word?
The thing you use to measure your temperature. What is it called again?
A thermometer! See -- I understood you perfectly without the exact word.
Right? A year ago I would have gone tongue-tied and given up.
And now you just keep going. What helped you the most, do you think?
A calm pace, and not being afraid of mistakes. I express the idea and keep the conversation moving.
That is real fluency. I am proud of you -- you got your whole story across beautifully.
What I mean is...
Give me a second to think.
Can you tell me more about that?
So, what about you?
Anyway, as I was saying...

Here is the secret that took me years to learn: fluency is not about knowing every word. It is about what you do when you do not know one. A fluent speaker does not freeze. If a word is missing, they describe it -- they say the thing you use for cooking, or the place where you buy bread -- and they keep going. They speak at a calm, steady pace, they are not afraid of small mistakes, and they always keep the conversation moving. So the next time a word disappears from your head, do not stop and translate. Paraphrase, breathe, and carry on. That is how you sustain a real conversation, and that is what real fluency sounds like.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I just had to call and say congratulations. Do you remember our very first classes, when you translated every single sentence in your head before you spoke? Look at you now -- you finished the whole program, and last week you talked to your colleague for ten minutes without stopping once. I know you still make little mistakes, but honestly, so do I, and so does everyone. The point is, you keep the conversation going, you get your ideas across, and you actually enjoy speaking now. That is the real breakthrough. I am so proud of you. Let us celebrate soon. Call me back when you can. Bye!

A year ago, I translated every sentence in my head. I stumbled on hard words and often felt tongue-tied. Then I stopped waiting for the perfect sentence. When I forgot a word, I described it and kept going. Last week I spoke for ten minutes without translating, and that was my breakthrough.

I can now sustain a conversation for several minutes.
When I forget a word, I express the idea in other words.
I used to feel tongue-tied in every meeting.
Speaking for ten minutes was a real breakthrough for me.
Try to speak at a calm, steady pace.
I got my point across without any trouble.
I can keep the conversation going, even when I forget a word.
What I mean is, I describe it in other words.
Give me a second to think, and then I will answer.
I made mistakes, but I got my point across.
What I mean is...
Let me think for a second.
Can you tell me more about that?
So, what about you?
Anyway, as I was saying...
